topic: Implementing 2D and 3D Perlin noise to create organic textures and procedural animations

concepts.name: Perlin Noise
concepts.explanation: A type of noise that produces organic, non-random patterns where each value is mathematically related to its neighbors, unlike pure random functions.
concepts.why_it_matters: It is essential for creating natural-looking textures like clouds, mountains, or terrain instead of chaotic white noise.

concepts.name: 2D Noise
concepts.explanation: Expanding noise from a single dimension to two dimensions by passing both x and y coordinates into the noise function.
concepts.why_it_matters: Allows for creating spatial textures (like a map) rather than just linear gradients.

concepts.name: Temporal Animation (3D Noise)
concepts.explanation: Adding a third dimension (z) to the noise function and incrementing it over time to create movement.
concepts.why_it_matters: Enables procedural animation, making textures like clouds or water appear to flow or change over time.

concepts.name: Noise Scaling
concepts.explanation: Dividing the input coordinates (e.g., x/100) to control the rate of change; smaller divisors make it more chaotic, larger divisors make it smoother.
concepts.why_it_matters: It determines the 'granularity' or frequency of the pattern.

code_examples.demonstrates: Creating a 2D grid of rectangles where the color is determined by spatial noise.
code_examples.common_issue: If you forget to divide the input (e.g., just using noise(x, y)), the transitions will be too fast and look like pure random static.

code_examples.demonstrates: Using a third parameter (z) to animate the noise pattern over time.
code_examples.common_issue: If you don't increment z, the image will remain static even though it is using 3D noise.

mistakes_and_fixes.symptom: The noise only changes horizontally (vertically striped pattern)
mistakes_and_fixes.cause: Only passing the x coordinate to the noise function: `noise(x/100)`
mistakes_and_fixes.fix: Add the y coordinate as a second parameter: `noise(x/100, y/100)`

mistakes_and_fixes.symptom: The computer is very slow or the animation lags
mistakes_and_fixes.cause: Setting the `size` variable too small (e.g., 1), which forces the computer to calculate noise for every single pixel.
mistakes_and_fixes.fix: Increase the `size` variable to create a coarser grid and reduce computational load.

mistakes_and_fixes.symptom: The animation isn't moving even though I added z to the noise function
mistakes_and_fixes.cause: Forgetting to increment the z variable (e.g., `z += 0.01`) at the end of the draw loop.
mistakes_and_fixes.fix: Add an increment to the z variable every frame.

key_moments.description: Introduction of 'fractal thinking'—mapping noise values to height (Z) to create landscapes.
Warning about computational expense when using very small grid sizes.
Live troubleshooting/observation of how changing the grid size affects visual complexity.

student_questions: Why is my noise just a bunch of vertical stripes?
How do I make the pattern move like clouds?
Why is my computer lagging when I use noise?
How do I make the noise look smoother or more 'blobby'?
What is the difference between random() and noise()?
How do I use noise to make a 3D landscape?
Why are all my colors just grayscale?
How do I make the noise change faster or slower?
Can I use noise for more than just color?
How do I make a texture that isn't just static?
